dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond for a motor vehicle strike report at Richmond--Address Redacted--.
My name is Derek, and I'm an EMT. What happened today that brought us to you, sir?
It's down here, my back. And also my right hip; it's hurting a lot, really bad right now.
Can you tell me your name for me?
I'm Matt.
Where are we now, Matt?
Yeah, Richmond, I think. I'm not exactly sure what street we're on, though.
Do you mind if we take a quick look at your airway to see how you're breathing right now, sir?
He has clear breath sounds in both lungs-no difficulty in breathing noticed yet.
His pulse is strong and regular at 80 per minute. His skin looks pink and dry all over, and he's warm to the touch.
Can you describe how this started for you, exactly? Any symptoms kick in before or after you got hit?

dialogue.role: events leading up to the event; Bystander
dialogue.utterance: Right, he was thrown up in the air and landed flat on his back on the street right here. We were right here watching it happen!

dialogue.role: events leading up to the event; Patient
dialogue.utterance: This car-black sedan-came out of nowhere accelerating straight into me. I got startled and ran into its side. It pushed me in and I just got thrown out.
On a scale from 0 to 10 with 10 being the worst pain you've ever had, how would you rate your back and hip pain, sir?
Definitely 10-this is the worst pain I've ever had in my life right now. I can't move without feeling it more.
Can I check your skin to look for bruises, cuts, or any unusual spots so we get a full view, sir?
He has some track marks-you know, small needle marks-on his arms, right over the veins. Elsewhere, skin appears pink, dry, and warm. No obvious injuries noted at the moment.
His pupils are both equal and reactive.
May I feel around your stomach and sides for any tenderness or signs of discomfort before we move up to your back?
He experiences a lot of discomfort with movement, but no obvious deformities are seen beyond the right hip. His legs and arms all feel responsive, and he has equal pedal pulses.
His pulse is 80 now, normal and strong all the way through.
His respiratory rate is 20 breaths per minute, normal and controlled as well. He's breathing regularly right now.
His blood pressure is at 170 over 97. That's standing strong with no changes for now.
His oxygen level is 100%. That's very good, at full air saturation now.
His glucose level is currently not measurable in the field with standard equipment-will monitor in the hospital if needed.
We're trying to insert a new IV into his left arm now. We'll need to make sure he's hooked up for any fluids or meds en route.

We're placing an 18-gauge IV line into his right arm now to establish proper access. Got it in. Draining some blood through it and will flush with saline in a few moments. Got that first IV started successfully.
Now we're hooking up this 4-lead monitor to his chest. We'll be able to track his EKG consistently en route.
Pulse jumped to 108 now. That's a bit faster, need to keep an eye on it as we go on. His HR went up, possibly due to pain.
His breathing rate also has increased slightly to 22 a minute now. Likely due to the same condition.
His BP is now at 178 over 122. Systolic is still strong, but the diastolic is higher now.
His SpO₂ dropped slightly to 80. We have to monitor that closely.
I just placed another IV after the failed initial attempt, on the right arm again-ready for normal saline flush looking to stabilize for transport.
His HR is now slower at 108 compared to the initial readings and remains elevated. Possible pain-related tachycardia is ongoing.
His BP is now at 178 over 122-that's a slight increase in the diastolic but still within the normal range for a trauma patient.
His SpO₂ has climbed back up to 92%. That's an improvement from earlier measurements.
The rhythm seen on the EKG now is sinus arrhythmia-no cardiac issues found at this time.
Given the traffic incident with mid to lower back and right hip pain and two IVs established along with EKG monitoring, we're following injury - general trauma management (Protocol 4-1).
We're going to VCU Health Systems for evaluation of potential back and pelvis trauma.
Securing the backboard and x-collar now with spider straps. The patient is being log-rolled with assistance. We are prepping for safe ambulance transfer.
I am calling VCU ED with report on the way via my cell phone now-will update when we arrive to Trauma Bay Bed 4.
Patient's airway remains patent and self-maintained, no intervention required at this time.
Patient's breathing is stable with no distress at this time.
Patient's radial pulse is strong and regular, skin is warm, pink, and dry, and SpO₂ has improved to 92%.
Patient's abdomen is soft and non-tender.
The patient is oriented and able to identify person, place, time, and event and is alert.

Possible deformity is noted to the right hip, though it is unclear if it is from the injury or just due to patient discomfort and movement. We are securing him for transport and following protocol 4-1.
We will proceed to VCU Health Systems Emergency Department for further evaluation and treatment.
Patient loaded into ambulance, secured on backboard with full spine precautions. Spinal stabilization is in place with approved immobilization devices.
